tested_outcome: Production-ready second pass. First version was beautiful but ambiguous about the product. Second version with clarity rule landed perfectly — premium feel intact, product unmistakeably named.

input_context.headline: THE QUIET LUXURY OF WHOLE-HOME DUCTED A/C
input_context.headline_accent_phrase: QUIET LUXURY (in italic serif accent typeface)
input_context.sub_headline: Whole-home ducted heating & cooling, designed for Newcastle homes.
input_context.value_stack: Whisper-Quiet Operation | Smart Phone Control | Whole-Home Climate | 7-Year Manufacturer Warranty

assets_attached.instruction: Match premium positioning, refined typography, aspirational feel

assets_attached.asset: Family lifestyle photo (Australian home interior, candid, faces visible)
assets_attached.instruction: Use AS-IS, do not modify people/setting

key_pivot_from_first_attempt: First attempt: 'THE QUIET LUXURY EVERY ROOM DESERVES' — too ambiguous, didn't name the product. Bray flagged: scrollers couldn't categorise the ad in the first second. Fix: 'THE QUIET LUXURY OF WHOLE-HOME DUCTED A/C' — kept the elegance/curiosity but added the product name explicitly.
composition_choices_that_worked: Two-half split: text-led left, photo-led right — premium magazine feel
'QUIET LUXURY' in italic serif, rest in bold sans-serif — typographic contrast does the elegance work
Sub-headline does double duty: names the product AGAIN (clarity safety net) AND personalises to city
TWO brand logos only (not three) — fewer logos = more premium
Subtle airflow vector arrows above ceiling vent in photo — adds product context without clutter
Cream/navy/soft gold palette emerged from AI — restrained, no bargain colours
PREMIUM DESIGN-INSTALL badge sized properly (not decorative) — looks intentional
No price anywhere — the offer is the consultation, not a number
60px safe-zone padding maintained — CTA, badge, brand strip all properly inside the canvas
what_failed_first_time_and_was_fixed: First attempt: 'THE QUIET LUXURY EVERY ROOM DESERVES' — evocative but didn't name product. Confused viewers. Premium aesthetic but unclear what's for sale.
Fix: added 'OF WHOLE-HOME DUCTED A/C' to headline + named product in sub-headline + made airflow lines slightly more visible
Lesson: clarity beats cleverness even in premium ads. Big brands can be cryptic because they have brand recognition. Direct-response installers cannot.
Also: first attempt had 'PREMIUM DESIGN-INSTALL' too small — felt like decorative footnote not meaningful element. Fixed by explicitly briefing 'meaningful element, not decorative footnote'.
full_prompt_used:
You are being given two attached images:

IMAGE 1 (reference ad): Use this as a strong style and layout reference. Match its premium positioning, refined typography, and aspirational feel — but DO NOT copy its headline, family photo, or specific colours. Generate fresh ones per the brief below.

IMAGE 2 (lifestyle photo): Use this image AS-IS for the lifestyle hero. Place it in the ad — do not modify the people, the setting, or the lighting. You may apply a very subtle palette adjustment if needed for cohesion, but do not alter what's in the photo.

Create a square 1080×1080 social media ad for a residential ducted air conditioning installer in Newcastle, Australia.

FUNNEL CONTEXT
This ad is for a cold/warm premium audience — buyers in the aspirational mindset, not the bargain mindset. The job is to elevate ducted air conditioning as a desirable home upgrade, not push a discount. No urgency, no aggressive pricing — but the product category must remain instantly clear.

CRITICAL CLARITY RULE
A scroller must understand within ONE SECOND that this ad is for DUCTED AIR CONDITIONING. The category must be unmissable. Premium aesthetic does not override this — clarity always beats cleverness. The product name must appear clearly in either the headline, the sub-headline, or both.

MOOD & STYLE
Refined, sophisticated, aspirational. Choose a fresh elegant colour palette of your own — interiors-magazine, premium-real-estate, modern-home-brand territory. Avoid bargain-bin colour combinations. Pick typography that mixes a strong modern sans-serif for the body with one elegant accent typeface (italic serif works well) for one or two key words. Generous breathing room around all elements.

LAYOUT (left half / right half split)

LEFT HALF — text-led
1. TOP BADGE
Clearly readable badge upper-left: "PREMIUM DESIGN-INSTALL" uppercase. Must be large enough to read at thumbnail size — NOT tiny. Meaningful element, not decorative footnote.

2. HEADLINE — left half, vertically centred
Stacked: "THE QUIET LUXURY OF WHOLE-HOME DUCTED A/C"
"QUIET LUXURY" set in elegant accent typeface (italic serif works beautifully) — visual signature. Rest in oversized bold sans-serif. Three or four lines stacked. "DUCTED A/C" must be clearly visible — do NOT shrink it.

3. SUB-HEADLINE
Italic accent text, clearly readable: "Whole-home ducted heating & cooling, designed for Newcastle homes."

4. VALUE STACK
Four refined tick-bullets, generous spacing:
   ✓ Whisper-Quiet Operation
   ✓ Smart Phone Control
   ✓ Whole-Home Climate
   ✓ 7-Year Manufacturer Warranty

5. CTA BUTTON — bottom-left
Refined rounded pill: "BOOK A DESIGN CONSULTATION"
Medium uppercase. Understated colour — invitation, not hard sell. Meaningful size, not footer element.

RIGHT HALF — visual hero
6. LIFESTYLE PHOTO
Attached photo as hero. Right half, full-height. Cinematic, aspirational. Do NOT modify people or setting.

7. SUBTLE AIRFLOW VECTOR ARROWS
Above the people, very subtle thin curving lines suggesting climate flow downward. Barely visible — refined design touch, not heavy graphic.

BOTTOM STRIP
8. Two brand logos, evenly spaced, native colours: Daikin, Mitsubishi Heavy Industries. Just TWO — premium positioning is built on fewer logos. Sit on contrasting band at bottom edge.

GLOBAL HARD RULES
- 1080×1080
- Lifestyle photo AS PROVIDED — do not modify people, do not regenerate faces
- Do NOT include any pricing — no per-week, no fixed, no "from $X". This is no-price premium variant.
- Do NOT include "0% interest" or "interest-free" or "discount" or "sale" or "off"
- Do NOT add discount badges or urgency timers
- Do NOT add review badges
- Maximum 2 brand logos
- Generous breathing room — NOT dense or cluttered
- "QUIET LUXURY" visually distinct from rest of headline (italic serif treatment)
- "DUCTED A/C" clearly visible, sized as meaningful part of headline
- Maintain 60px safe-zone padding inside all four edges
- Every text element readable at thumbnail size
lessons_for_layer_2: CLARITY RULE is non-negotiable — premium ads must still name the product. Belt-and-braces: name in headline AND sub-headline.
Two-half layout (text left, photo right) is the standard premium pattern — avoids the dense direct-response feel
Italic serif accent on 1-2 key words is the elegance tell — works in any premium ad
Premium positioning uses FEWER logos (2 not 3), no urgency, no badges with discounts, no prices
Sub-headline is the safety net — when the headline tries to be evocative, the sub-headline carries the clarity load
Always size badges/labels as 'meaningful elements not decorative footnotes' — the AI defaults to too small unless told
